children.text: This is when I noticed the texture starting to build. At about a minute and a half into the recording, it sounds like strings begin to scratch and cracks begin to form in the recording. Splashes. The sound of water. Cracks forming. The layers of each sound start to build on one another.

The background texture seems to develop a little bit of a more active presence through the resonant and hollow hum. I think what feels active about this sound is that it changes my experience of the tension between the high-pitched strings and the screeching in the background that has been building since minute one. The timing at which this resonant and hollow hum enters feels important, a break in the building tension.

The screeching turns to screaming. It builds from 10:44 and drops off at 11:29. The experience is pretty overwhelming. This partnership between the hydrophone recording and "screaming" comes through this really specific sound of ice moving and cracking and rubbing and growing-- it's difficult to explain. See annotation at 11:35.
Building on the last annotation, this mix between mic and instrument feels very unclear to me. The slippage of ice feels also like a slippage in instrument.
